sections.body: The estimating workflow starts before any takeoff. Commercial estimators receive 2-10x more invitations than they can realistically bid, so step one is qualification: project size, owner type, schedule realism, and competitor field. Bid management tools like BuildingConnected, iSqFt, and SmartBid aggregate invitations and let estimators flag promising ones early.
sections.heading: 1. Bid solicitation & qualification

sections.body: Once a job is in, drawings get organized by trade, sheet, and revision. Bluebeam Revu is the long-standing market choice for marking up and comparing drawing sets; cloud tools like Procore and Autodesk Construction Cloud now compete here too. Version discipline matters — a single estimate built on superseded drawings can blow a margin.
sections.heading: 2. Drawing intake & versioning

sections.body: Digital takeoff is now standard. PlanSwift, Stack, On-Screen Takeoff, and Esticom all offer point-and-click measurement against PDFs and DWGs. For BIM-driven jobs, Sigma Estimates and CostX pull quantities directly from the model — eliminating an entire round of human counting.
sections.heading: 3. Quantity takeoff

sections.body: Quantities become dollars via assembly databases. RSMeans provides localized, regularly updated unit cost data. Estimating platforms like ProEst, HCSS HeavyBid, and Sage Estimating layer assemblies, labor productivity rates, and equipment cost on top.
sections.heading: 4. Pricing & assembly

sections.body: Self-perform line items get priced internally; everything else goes out to subs. SmartBid, BuildingConnected, and Pantera Tools handle ITB distribution, qualification, and quote collection — keeping a paper trail you'll need post-award.
sections.heading: 5. Subcontractor solicitation

sections.body: In the final hours, sub quotes get pasted into the schedule of values. Markup gets adjusted by gut and by GC strategy. Spreadsheets still dominate this final step; teams using Destini Estimator, ProEst, or Sigma get a more controlled workflow with audit history.
sections.heading: 6. Bid day & final scrub

sections.body: Won the job? The estimate becomes the project budget. Tools like Procore and Sage 300 CRE pull the structured estimate into the production system so PMs can track real spend vs estimate — closing the feedback loop on which assumptions were right.
sections.heading: 7. Handoff to operations

workflows.outro: The estimating workflow is roughly the same shape at every commercial contractor — what differs is how much of it lives in disciplined software vs scattered spreadsheets. Most teams improve fastest by tightening steps 3 (takeoff) and 5 (sub solicitation) first.
workflows.title: How Commercial Contractors Estimate Projects
workflows.intro: Commercial estimating is fundamentally different from residential — it's a process problem, not a calculation problem. Here's how high-performing commercial contractors actually run the estimating workflow, and which tools they reach for at each stage.

sections.body: Calls arrive via phone, web booking, and recurring maintenance triggers. Modern HVAC software (ServiceTitan, FieldEdge, Housecall Pro) captures all three into one queue with priority tagging.
sections.heading: 1. Call intake & priority

sections.body: Dispatchers see today's board — assigned techs, current job, ETA, and skill tags. Drag-and-drop interfaces let them snap a new call onto the right tech in seconds. Synchroteam, Workiz, and ServiceFusion all support visual scheduling.
sections.heading: 2. Schedule preview

sections.body: Not every HVAC tech is licensed for boilers. Not every tech installs mini-splits. Smart dispatch tools tag techs by certification and only show qualified options for each call.
sections.heading: 3. Tech assignment by skill

sections.body: The biggest customer-experience lever is "tech is 12 minutes out" SMS. Podium, Housecall Pro, and ServiceTitan all automate this — and the conversion lift on the next service call is measurable.
sections.heading: 4. Customer ETA notifications

sections.body: GPS plus dashcams (Samsara, Verizon Connect, Motive) protect against insurance claims and let dispatchers re-route the right tech if a call slips.
sections.heading: 5. Live tech tracking

sections.body: Tech wraps the call, gets a signature on the phone, and the invoice fires automatically. Same platforms close the loop with QuickBooks or accounting integrations.
sections.heading: 6. Job completion → invoice

workflows.outro: HVAC dispatch maturity follows a predictable arc: paper → spreadsheets → FSM software → integrated suite with telematics. The biggest jump in throughput typically happens at step 3.
workflows.title: How HVAC Contractors Dispatch Technicians
workflows.intro: HVAC dispatching is a real-time optimization problem: skill match, ETA, travel time, capacity, and customer urgency all matter. Here's the modern workflow.

sections.body: Before tooling, map your stages: web lead → qualified → scheduled → on-site → quoted → won → invoiced → reviewed. Each stage needs an owner and a service-level expectation.
sections.heading: 1. Define the lead lifecycle

sections.body: For 1-5 truck operations, Jobber and Housecall Pro are the natural fits. For 5-30 trucks, Workiz and FieldEdge add power. Beyond that, ServiceTitan dominates.
sections.heading: 2. Pick the right platform

sections.body: Web form → CRM. Phone → call tracking (CallRail) → CRM. Recurring maintenance → automated trigger → dispatch.
sections.heading: 3. Wire up your lead sources

sections.body: The single biggest revenue lever is a flat-rate pricebook on every truck. ServiceTitan and Profit Rhino are common; Housecall Pro and Jobber have lighter-weight equivalents.
sections.heading: 4. Build flat-rate pricebook

sections.body: Review request, thank-you note, and warranty reminder. NiceJob, Podium, and Broadly handle this with one-click integrations.
sections.heading: 5. Automate post-job touchpoints

sections.body: Look at win rate by lead source, average ticket, and customer lifetime value. CRMs with built-in dashboards (ServiceTitan, Housecall Pro) make this trivial.
sections.heading: 6. Track and refine

workflows.outro: The plumbers winning right now aren't the ones with the best techs — they're the ones whose CRM workflow turns every call into a tracked, optimized event.
workflows.title: How Plumbing Companies Set Up a CRM Workflow
workflows.intro: A plumbing CRM isn't just a contact list — it's a lead-to-cash workflow with dispatching, on-truck quoting, and recurring service triggers built in.

sections.body: Every sub gets a defined contract value, schedule of values (SOV), and retainage terms. Procore, RedTeam, and Sage 300 CRE all model this natively.
sections.heading: 1. Subcontract setup

sections.body: Subs submit pay apps against the SOV. Procore Invoicing, GCPay, and Textura (now Oracle) are common platforms. DocuSign streamlines signatures.
sections.heading: 2. Monthly billing intake

sections.body: Conditional + unconditional lien waivers tracked per billing cycle. Missing waivers stall payment — and create real legal exposure.
sections.heading: 3. Lien waiver collection

sections.body: PM verifies work-in-place vs billed-to-date. Procore and RedTeam route through approvers automatically.
sections.heading: 4. PM approval workflow

sections.body: Accounting integrates with Sage 300 CRE, Foundation Software, QuickBooks Enterprise, or CMiC to issue checks/ACH.
sections.heading: 5. Accounting cut & release

sections.body: Year-end reporting needs clean records. The platforms above all generate the necessary reports.
sections.heading: 6. Audit trail & 1099s

workflows.outro: A clean sub-payment workflow protects margin, protects the schedule (subs that get paid on time show up), and protects against liens. The platforms above pay for themselves on a single mid-size project.
workflows.title: How General Contractors Manage Subcontractor Payments
workflows.intro: Sub-payment workflows are where projects bleed cash quietly. Here's the disciplined process modern GCs follow.

sections.body: Start with one platform that handles scheduling, dispatching, quoting, and invoicing. Jobber or Housecall Pro for most trades. Skip enterprise FSM until you're past 10 employees.
sections.heading: 1. Field service / CRM (week 1)

sections.body: QuickBooks Online. Connect it to your FSM the same week. Don't wait — backfilling accounting is brutal.
sections.heading: 2. Accounting (week 1)

sections.body: Mobile card processing built into your FSM. Stripe or Square as backup. Offer financing via Payzer if your average ticket is over $1k.
sections.heading: 3. Payments (week 2)

sections.body: NiceJob is the easiest entry. Podium if you want SMS-first. Don't skip this — your Google rating is your CAC.
sections.heading: 4. Reviews (week 2)

sections.body: Slack for the team, Google Workspace for email + docs. Skip Microsoft 365 unless you already use it.
sections.heading: 5. Communication (week 3)

sections.body: ClockShark or Connecteam — both have geofencing and clean QuickBooks integration.
sections.heading: 6. Time tracking (month 2)

sections.body: Mailchimp for email lists, Canva for graphics, WordPress for the website. Total stack cost: ~$50/mo combined.
sections.heading: 7. Marketing (month 3)

workflows.outro: Total monthly cost for this entire stack runs roughly $200-400/mo at the small-business scale. Add tools as the business grows — don't front-load.
workflows.title: Building a Small-Contractor Tech Stack from Scratch
workflows.intro: You're a 1-5 employee contractor. Here's the minimum viable software stack — what to buy, what to skip, and the order to deploy.

sections.body: Two-way SMS confirmation at the moment of booking. ServiceTitan and Housecall Pro send this automatically; smaller-stack teams can use Podium.
sections.heading: 1. Confirm at booking

sections.body: Automated SMS + email day-before. Re-confirm appointment window. Offer easy reschedule link.
sections.heading: 2. 24-hour reminder

sections.body: "Tech is on the way" SMS with tech name and photo. This single touch reduces no-shows by 30%+ in practice.
sections.heading: 3. Day-of dispatch SMS

sections.body: Customer sees the truck approaching, just like an Uber. ServiceTitan, FieldEdge, and Housecall Pro all support this.
sections.heading: 4. Live GPS tracking

sections.body: Automated SMS within 30 minutes asking what happened. About 1 in 3 reschedules vs being lost to a competitor.
sections.heading: 5. Follow-up on no-shows

workflows.outro: Steps 3 and 4 are the highest-leverage. Most teams implement step 2 first because it's easy — but step 3 moves the needle hardest.
workflows.title: How Field Service Teams Reduce No-Shows and Reschedules
workflows.intro: Every no-show is roughly $200-500 in lost revenue and crew time. Here's the operational workflow top performers use to cut no-show rate by 50-70%.

sections.body: Convert the winning estimate into the project budget at award. Procore, Sage 300 CRE, and Foundation Software all import structured estimates from ProEst, Sage Estimating, or HeavyBid.
sections.heading: 1. Set the budget at award

sections.body: Every field hour must hit a cost code. ClockShark, Rhumbix, or the time-tracking module in Procore handle this on the phone.
sections.heading: 2. Code time to cost codes

sections.body: Every invoice from a supplier or sub also hits a cost code. Sage 300 CRE, Foundation Software, and Procore Financials make this routine.
sections.heading: 3. AP coding

sections.body: Weekly, PM reviews current spend, work-in-place, and forecasts remaining. The variance between estimate and forecast is your true margin signal.
sections.heading: 4. Live cost-to-complete forecasting

sections.body: At 25%, 50%, 75% completion: full margin review. Adjust forecasts. Lock in change orders. Catch budget bleed before it's irreversible.
sections.heading: 5. Margin review at milestone

workflows.outro: The single most overlooked step is #4 — many teams only review profitability at project close, which is far too late to act.
workflows.title: How Commercial Contractors Track Project Profitability
workflows.intro: Project-level profitability is the single number that separates contractors who scale from contractors who go bust. Here's the workflow to track it in real time.

sections.body: Web form, phone, walk-in referrals, Google Maps, Yelp, Angi, Thumbtack — all into one queue. CallRail for call tracking, your CRM for everything else.
sections.heading: 1. Capture every lead, every channel

sections.body: Without source tagging, you can't calculate channel ROI. Most contractor CRMs have a one-click source field. Use it ruthlessly.
sections.heading: 2. Tag lead source on every record

sections.body: Inbound web lead → SMS to dispatcher within 60 seconds. Speed-to-lead is the #1 conversion lever in trade services.
sections.heading: 3. Auto-route to dispatcher

sections.body: Tablet-based flat-rate pricebook. Same options shown to every customer. Conversion rate goes up; price erosion goes down.
sections.heading: 4. Standardize the in-home quote

sections.body: Day 1, day 3, day 7, day 30 — automated SMS + email. About 15% of "lost" quotes will book within 30 days from this sequence alone.
sections.heading: 5. Follow-up sequence on unwon quotes

sections.body: Annual maintenance reminders, water heater anniversaries, HVAC seasonal tune-ups. Set once, runs forever.
sections.heading: 6. Recurring service triggers

workflows.outro: Most contractors get to step 2 and stall. The compounding returns happen at steps 5 and 6 — and they're the cheapest to implement.
workflows.title: The Best CRM Workflow for Contractor Businesses
workflows.intro: Generic CRM advice doesn't apply to contractors. Your sales process happens in the truck. Here's the workflow tailored to how trade businesses actually operate.
